parts.content: Identify a scenario where a software developer would find it more advantageous to implement a **bubble sort** rather than a **selection sort**, justifying the selection with two reasons.
parts.markscheme:
- A dataset that is already known to be nearly sorted; <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Justifications:**
- Bubble sort can achieve a best-case time complexity of $O(n)$ (if a swap flag is used) for already sorted data, whereas selection sort remains $O(n^2)$; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bubble sort is stable (does not change the relative order of equal elements), whereas selection sort is typically not stable; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bubble sort can terminate early when no swaps occur (using a swap flag), whereas selection sort still performs the full pass to find a minimum each time; <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Identify the values that would be displayed by the following algorithm when processed with the input sequence: $15, 28, 18, 22, 25, 30, 12, 19$

```text
K = 1
while K <= 8 do
    get VAL
    if VAL > 20 then
        if VAL mod 2 = 0 then
            display VAL + 5
        end if
    end if
    K = K + 1
end while
```
parts.markscheme:
- State the first correct value displayed: $33$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the second correct value displayed: $27$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the third correct value displayed: $35$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Explain how a **binary search** algorithm reduces the number of elements to be checked during each iteration.
parts.markscheme:
<markscheme>
- The algorithm identifies the middle element of the current search range and compares it to the target value <marks>A1</marks>
- It then discards the half of the range where the target cannot exist (based on the sorted order), effectively halving the search space each time <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total
</markscheme>

parts.content: Outline **two** ways in which **decomposition** aids a programmer when designing a complex system.
parts.markscheme:
<markscheme>
Award 1 mark for any of the following, up to 2 marks:
- It breaks a complex problem into smaller, more manageable sub-tasks that are easier to understand and code <marks>A1</marks>
- It allows different parts of the system to be developed and tested independently by different team members <marks>A1</marks>
- It helps in identifying specific procedures that can be reused in other parts of the program or future projects <marks>A1</marks>
- It makes the final system easier to maintain as errors can be localized to specific sub-modules <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total
</markscheme>

parts.content:
An analyst is dry-running an algorithm designed to find the highest value from a list of five temperature records. Prepare a trace table to show the execution of the following algorithm.

```text
SAMPLES = [40, 38, 42, 45, 39]
MAX = SAMPLES[0]
I = 1
while I < 5
   if SAMPLES[I] > MAX then
      MAX = SAMPLES[I]
   end if
   I = I + 1
end while
output MAX
```
parts.markscheme:
- Create a trace table with appropriate headers (I, SAMPLES[I], I < 5, SAMPLES[I] > MAX, MAX, and output) and initial values (I=1, MAX=40) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly trace the values of I and the corresponding array elements SAMPLES[I] for each iteration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly evaluate the loop condition I < 5 for each step, including the final False result when I = 5 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly evaluate the conditional SAMPLES[I] > MAX and update the variable MAX only when the condition is True (at I=2 and I=3) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identify the final value of MAX and the single output of 45 after the loop terminates <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Trace Table:**

| I | SAMPLES[I] | I < 5 | SAMPLES[I] > MAX | MAX | output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|   |   |   |   | 40 |   |
| 1 | 38 | True | False | 40 |   |
| 2 | 42 | True | True | 42 |   |
| 3 | 45 | True | True | 45 |   |
| 4 | 39 | True | False | 45 |   |
| 5 |   | False |   |   | 45 |

5 marks total

topic.title: 4.2 Connecting computational thinking and program design
